text: Welcome to This is My Architecture.
I'm Christopher from AWS, and today I'm joined by Luis from iRobot.
Welcome.
Hi.
Excellent.
So what does iRobot do?
iRobot makes IoT connected robots.
Excellent.
And what are you doing with this pattern in conjunction with iRobot?
Yeah, so this is our data lake architecture that's used to,
basically for research scientists to explore and find interesting patterns in our data.
Excellent.
So how does it all begin?
Yeah, so from our data pipeline, we have JSON records right into S3.
And that data then gets cataloged by the glue crawler,
and that information gets stored in the data catalog.
What kind of information is stored in the data?
So in the catalog, you can see things like column type, column name, table name.
And that basically allows you to inspect the schema of the information coming in.
Excellent.
And so once that data's into the schema itself, what happens then?
So the JSON data, you can query it already using Athena.
And there's about three terabytes of data coming in a day.
So typically, you'd only really want to query a couple hours or even one day of data using that service.
So in order to support full history querying, we end up converting the data from JSON records into Parquet format.
And how did you do that?
So the glue crawler will take in the data that has recently arrived.
Yep.
The glue job will take in the data that has recently arrived, and will write that data as Parquet into the S3 bucket.
And so Parquet is over here now.
So what's the value of Parquet over JSON?
So Parquet is a columnar format, data format.
So that allows the query engine to selectively scan information that's in the, to meet the needs of your query.
And it effectively ends up saving you money and time.
Okay. So it's passing a smaller subset of data in that respect.
Okay. And does this get crawled as well and put into the data catalog?
Yeah. The glue crawler will also catalog the Parquet files, and that will also, that information will also be stored in the data catalog.
Excellent. So as a company, what benefits are you gaining from actually having this ecosystem?
Yeah. So this ecosystem allows us to very quickly find specific production bugs, either in a particular robot or all robots.
It also allows us to build new features off of the data that we're collecting, and basically helping improve the customer experience.
So you have the immediate search here on the JSON, and you have the slightly more extended timeline when it comes to the Parquet itself.
Yeah. Excellent. And this complete pattern, does it have any physical characteristics, or is it completely serverless?
The whole architecture is completely serverless.
Excellent. So that's a really useful one.
Yeah.
Well, listen, this is a great ecosystem, so thank you very much for bringing this into the day.
Thanks.
Excellent.
And thank you for joining us. This is my architecture.
